title: Coverage matrix p95 10-min budget may miss on 500+ MB packs due to OCR+inference cascade
feature: Coverage matrix generation (100–500 MB evidence packs)
reasoning: Parent NFR anchors 10-minute p95 SLA for matrix generation, but OCR text extraction alone (180–360 s depending on pack size) consumes 30–50% of budget. Semantic classification inference adds 200–480 s. For 500 MB packs with high-resolution images and complex formatting, p95 OCR time may reach 400+ s, pushing combined inference to 900+ s (15+ min), blowing the 10-min SLA. Confidence elevated due to known OCR variance across document types.
recommendation: 1. Baseline OCR+inference latencies on representative 100/250/500 MB packs immediately. 2. Implement parallel OCR on multi-GPU infrastructure (stripe document by page). 3. Consider two-tier SLA: p95 10 min for ≤100 MB (fast path), p95 15–20 min for 100–500 MB (batch path). 4. Add per-pack size estimator to recommend async callback vs. blocking on intake.

title: Panel action log p95 500 ms SLA unachievable under contention due to immutable log quorum replication
reasoning: SLA targets 500 ms p95 for audit-log writes, but distributed quorum replication (consensus among 3+ nodes) typically requires 200–400 ms of network round-trips alone, plus 100+ ms for lock acquisition and classification inline checks. Under high panel contention (10+ simultaneous reviewers), lock wait times can exceed 300 ms, pushing tail latency to 800+ ms. Immutable log semantics (write-once, cryptographic hash chaining) add overhead that is difficult to amortize. Confidence is high based on known properties of consensus protocols (Raft, Paxos).
recommendation: 1. Re-baseline distributed log write latency (measure on staging with 3-node quorum). 2. Consider reducing quorum to 2 nodes for non-critical actions (accept eventual consistency risk). 3. Implement parallel action batching: buffer 10–20 ms of micro-actions, commit as single immutable record. 4. Upgrade SLA to p95 1 s for audit-log; p95 500 ms only for in-memory application state (non-audit writes). 5. Add horizontal scaling of log broker nodes to reduce contention.

title: AI-flag narrative p95 30-min budget at risk due to inference scaling and evidence chunking latency
reasoning: End-to-end p95 1800 s budget allocates 900 s to AI inference per-chunk. For a typical evidence pack with 100+ distinct sections (ConOps, SORA, Safety Case, Training, Personnel, Environmental, etc.), serial inference at 10–15 s per section results in 1000–1500 s baseline. Parallel inference across sections mitigates this, but requires reservation of GPU resources; contention during peak submission periods (e.g., near trial approval deadlines) may cause queuing delays of 300+ s. Additionally, evidence chunking strategy (semantic vs. sliding-window) and chunk boundary optimization add unpredictability. Confidence is moderate-to-high based on observed transformer inference latencies at scale.
recommendation: 1. Implement streaming narrative flag generation: show first-pass flags at 10 min, iterative refinement to 30 min. 2. Baseline inference latency per evidence section type (ConOps, SORA, Safety Case, etc.); create section-type-specific SLAs. 3. Reserve dedicated GPU capacity for narrative workload; implement priority queue (urgent cases bump lower-priority). 4. Measure end-to-end latency percentiles on a 100-case cohort; if p95 > 25 min, explore model quantization or distillation. 5. Add user-facing progress bar and ETA; set expectation that full narrative will be available within 30 min but first-pass flags appear sooner.

title: DAA collision resolution p95 5-s budget tight for non-cooperative multi-aircraft scenarios; 30-s target may not be conservative enough
reasoning: DO-365 collision avoidance SLA targets 5 s for cooperative encounters and 30 s for non-cooperative. However, airborne sensor fusion (radar, ADS-B, on-board vision), target classification, and swarm consensus coordination can easily consume 2–3 s even in nominal conditions. For non-cooperative encounters with ambiguous target classification (e.g., slow-moving balloon vs. small aircraft), sensor fusion confidence thresholds may extend classification time to 5–10 s. Swarm consensus protocol (negotiating avoidance maneuver among 3+ aircraft) adds another 1–2 s. Under worst-case (high sensor noise, GPS denial, multiple simultaneous encounters), p95 may reach 15+ s, falling short of the 30-s DO-365 target. Confidence is moderate based on published DO-365 guidance and empirical sensor fusion data.
recommendation: 1. Baseline DAA end-to-end latency on real-world sensor data (radar, ADS-B, optical). Measure p95 under nominal, degraded (GPS loss), and adversarial (spoof, noise) conditions. 2. Model encounter scenarios in simulation (cooperative, non-cooperative, ambiguous) and profile collision-resolution latency distribution. 3. If p95 exceeds 15 s for non-cooperative, implement hierarchical decision-making: fast solo response (5 s), then swarm consensus refinement (5–30 s). 4. Define sensor fusion confidence threshold (e.g., 95%) and corresponding classification SLA (must not exceed 3 s). 5. Add in-flight telemetry logging of encounter latency; use operational data to refine SLA annually.

title: Geofence containment p99 <500 ms (p99.9 < 1 s) may not account for C2 link jitter and radio transmission variance
feature: Geofence detection and action (p99 < 500 ms, hard deadline 1 s)
reasoning: Budget allocates 50 ms for GPS position update, 50 ms for boundary check, and 200 ms for C2 radio transmission. However, tactical C2 radio links (UHF/4G-LTE) exhibit jitter of 50–200 ms, especially in urban or GPS-denied environments. Additionally, GPS position update rate is typically 1–5 Hz (200–1000 ms between updates), introducing latency that is not accounted for in the budget. For a geofence violation detected just after a GPS update, the next position update (and hence detection) may be delayed 200+ ms. C2 transmission variance due to link quality, interference, or retransmission can add another 100+ ms tail latency. Combined, p99 could easily reach 600–800 ms, breaching the hard 1-s deadline in worst-case. Confidence is high based on known radio propagation and GPS sensor characteristics.
recommendation: 1. Implement airborne geofence monitoring at GPS update frequency (1–5 Hz); do not rely solely on ground-based detection. 2. Baseline GPS position update latency and variance on operational platform; use actual (not theoretical) frequencies. 3. Measure C2 link latency distribution under field conditions (not lab); characterize p50, p95, p99 transmission times. 4. Set geofence boundary margin (buffer zone) inversely proportional to C2 latency: margin_m = (c2_latency_s * swarm_speed_m_s) + safety_factor. 5. Validate containment logic in Monte Carlo simulation with realistic GPS/C2 jitter; confirm p99.9 < 1 s before flight trials.

title: Evidence upload SLA does not specify throughput for OFFICIAL vs. CONFIDENTIAL vs. UNCLASSIFIED evidence; classification overhead not budgeted
reasoning: Parent NFR specifies 30-s p95 for 100 MB pack under OFFICIAL classification, but the upload SLA does not differentiate latency for CONFIDENTIAL or UNCLASSIFIED evidence, nor does it account for mandatory inline classification validation. OFFICIAL evidence may require additional cryptographic checks (MOD classification marks, compartment validation) that add 100–500 ms per document. The current budget (3–5 MB/s net throughput) assumes classification is negligible, but may be violated if classification overhead is substantial. Additionally, no SLA is defined for hybrid packs that span multiple classification levels.
recommendation: 1. Measure classification-overhead latency per evidence type (ConOps, SORA, Safety Case, etc.) for OFFICIAL vs. CONFIDENTIAL vs. UNCLASSIFIED. 2. Define separate SLAs: (a) UNCLASSIFIED: p95 <30 s for 100 MB, (b) OFFICIAL: p95 <40 s for 100 MB, (c) hybrid pack: p95 <45 s (worst-case classification path). 3. Implement fast-track for UNCLASSIFIED (skip crypto checks); queue OFFICIAL/CONFIDENTIAL for async classification validator. 4. Add workbench UI warning: 'Uploads containing OFFICIAL evidence may take up to 40 s; use batch upload for efficiency.'

title: No latency SLA defined for Completeness Check (REQ001) beyond generic 'within submission workflow'; actual duration and acceptance criteria undefined
reasoning: Parent NFR REQ001 ('complete Operational Authorisation pack') and parent user story NFR ('Full workbench open-and-review cycle shall not require more than two working days') do not explicitly define a latency target for the Completeness Check step that gates case intake. Vibe Requirement PackCompleteness defines a scale (percentage of packs passing on first check) but no latency budget. Current NFR-LAT-005 targets Readiness Check (Step 4, post-upload) but there is a logical mismatch: intake-phase completeness validation (Step 1, synchronous) vs. in-panel readiness check (Step 4, asynchronous). Confidence is high that this gap exists.
recommendation: 1. Define explicit Completeness Check SLA for Step 1: 'p95 <10 s for intake-phase completeness screening; full document validation (PackCompleteness scale) p95 <60 s.' 2. Clarify: is Completeness Check a blocking gateway (hold submission until pass), or advisory (flag issues but allow proceeding)? 3. If blocking, allocate <10 s p95 for automated checks (file count, mandatory mime types, document header validation); defer manual review to asynchronous process. 4. Create acceptance criteria checklist for case officers ('Completeness Check pass = [list] all present').

title: No latency SLA for Phased Gates (F18) or trial progression workflow; 'two-day cycle' anchor is system-level, not step-level
feature: Phased Trial Gates & Progressive Evidence Build (F18, workflow orchestration)
reasoning: Parent NFR REQ016 ('phased gates so that evidence builds progressively and is reversible') and parent user story ('two-day cycle for representative swarm submission') do not break down step-level latency targets for gate progression (gate approval, evidence sign-off, phase transition). Current budgets focus on individual upload, matrix, and panel-action steps, but do not address the latency of gate state-machine transitions (approval decision propagation, evidence lock, phase unlock). Phased gate progression may be gated by human review latency (case officer decision time), which is not a system latency but may block downstream work.
recommendation: 1. Define gate progression SLAs: 'p95 gate approval decision rendered within 24 h of evidence submission.' 2. Separate system latency (gate state update: p95 <1 s) from human latency (approval decision: p95 <24 h). 3. Implement gate-readiness dashboard for case officers: 'Gate X ready for decision: [evidence summary, risk flags, AI recommendations].' 4. Add escalation SLA: 'If gate approval pending >24 h, escalate to supervisor.' 5. Track 'time in gate' metric per phased trial; use to refine two-day estimate.

title: No latency SLA for Security Classification Handling (F20) and MOD compartment validation; 'CAP 722H compliance' lacks measurable latency target
feature: Security Classification Handling (F20, MOD compartment validation)
reasoning: Parent NFR REQ018 ('classified MOD evidence is presented to the CAA under CAP 722H / JSP 440 without compromise') is a policy/compliance requirement, not a latency requirement. No SLA is defined for how quickly classified evidence must be validated, segregated, or made available to authorized reviewers. Classification validation (verifying MOD compartment markings, security clearance levels, need-to-know) may add 500+ ms per document (crypto validation, clearance database lookup). For a BVLOS swarm trial with classified avionics and autonomy evidence, this could introduce 5–10 s of latency per pack segment.
recommendation: 1. Define SLA: 'p95 classification validation (compartment check, clearance matching) <500 ms per document segment; p95 <30 s per 100 MB pack.' 2. Implement parallel classification processing (don't serialize on upload). 3. Pre-filter evidence at intake: separate OFFICIAL/CONFIDENTIAL from UNCLASSIFIED; route OFFICIAL to encrypted, access-controlled storage; render to clearance-appropriate reviewers only. 4. Add classification-status field to all workbench metadata: 'This evidence contains OFFICIAL compartment [XXXXXX]; access restricted to [role list].'

title: Coverage matrix generation latency can be improved 30–50% by splitting text extraction and inference into separate queue tiers
feature: Coverage matrix generation (OCR + semantic inference pipeline)
reasoning: Current end-to-end budget (NFR-LAT-003) allocates 180–360 s for OCR and 200–480 s for semantic inference in series. However, OCR is I/O-bound (disk read, image processing) while semantic inference is compute-bound (GPU); they can be parallelized. By decoupling, OCR can run on CPU workers while a separate batch of inference jobs (from prior pack uploads) runs on GPU. Additionally, OCR can be spun up on-demand across multiple machines (stateless, horizontal scale), reducing bottleneck risk. Confidence is high due to known workload characteristics of batch ML pipelines.
recommendation: 1. Refactor coverage matrix pipeline: (a) OCR tier: CPU-based document processing, output token stream to message queue; (b) Inference tier: GPU-based semantic classification, consume token stream from queue. 2. Scale OCR tier horizontally (auto-scale based on document queue depth). 3. Implement per-document OCR timeout (5 min/doc); if exceeded, report 'partial coverage' matrix and allow manual remediation. 4. Baseline latency reduction; target 30–50% improvement (10–15 min p95 for 500 MB pack). 5. Monitor queue depths and tier utilization; implement cost-aware scaling (turn down GPU when no pending inference).

title: Panel-action log write latency can be reduced by implementing write-behind buffering and eventual consistency for non-critical actions
reasoning: Current SLA (NFR-LAT-009) enforces synchronous quorum replication for all audit writes, driving p95 to 500 ms. However, not all actions require immediate quorum durability: transient UI state (panel scroll position, collapse/expand), non-binding review notes (drafts), and temporary flags can use write-behind buffering (write to local cache, async flush to quorum). Only binding actions (decision state changes, submitted verdicts, digital signatures) require immediate durability. This stratification can reduce average latency to <200 ms and still maintain audit integrity for critical actions.
recommendation: 1. Classify panel actions: (a) immediate-durability: decision verdicts, digital signatures, final flags (continue quorum SLA); (b) eventual-consistency: draft notes, temporary holds, UI state (implement write-behind, flush every 5–10 s or on 'save'). 2. Implement dual-write path: non-durable actions → local in-memory log + async flush; durable actions → sync quorum write. 3. Measure impact on user experience (perceived latency, usability of draft notes). 4. Add workbench audit report: distinguish between immediate and eventually-consistent actions. 5. Target p95 <200 ms for non-durable actions; p95 <1 s for durable actions.

title: Onboard ML inference p95 200 ms budget may not account for model load latency and GPU context switching on edge hardware
feature: Onboard ML inference for autonomy decisions
reasoning: Budget assumes 120 ms inference execution on already-loaded model, but edge avionics (Nvidia Jetson, commercial flight compute modules) may not pre-load large models in VRAM due to power/weight constraints. First inference after model swap can incur 500+ ms load latency. Additionally, context switching between multiple lightweight models (waypoint selector, DAA evasion, formation controller) can add 50+ ms per switch if not carefully orchestrated. Confidence is moderate based on empirical edge ML latencies and known avionics constraints.
recommendation: 1. Profile avionics hardware (CPU, GPU, thermal limits) and measure actual model load time for each autonomy model. 2. Implement model pre-loading strategy: keep all active models in VRAM (may require model quantization to fit). 3. Use single unified model or model ensemble where possible (reduce load/context-switching overhead). 4. Measure p95 inference latency on representative mission profiles (nominal transit, DAA encounter, loss-of-C2). 5. If p95 exceeds 250 ms, implement hierarchical response: (a) fast reactive model (<100 ms); (b) deliberative refinement (100–200 ms); queue re-planning for later cycle.

title: C2 link round-trip latency p95 <1 s may not be achievable on 4G/LTE spectrum with typical network congestion
reasoning: Budget allocates 300 ms each for uplink and downlink transmission. However, 4G/LTE networks exhibit highly variable latency depending on signal strength, congestion, and packet loss. Typical observed p95 latency on LTE is 50–200 ms per direction in ideal conditions, but can easily reach 500+ ms during peak hours or in fringe coverage areas. Additionally, retransmissions (TCP retransmit, link-layer ARQ) can add 100–1000 ms tail latency. For BVLOS swarms operating beyond visual range in varied terrain, C2 link outages and degradation are expected. Confidence is high based on published LTE network measurements and regulatory BVLOS trial data.
recommendation: 1. Measure actual C2 link latency on planned trial airspace (not lab); collect p50, p95, p99 during peak and off-peak hours. 2. Baseline on spectrum band and network carrier (LTE Band X, provider Y). 3. Re-evaluate SLA based on empirical data: if p95 <1 s is unachievable, set realistic target (e.g., p95 <2 s, p99 <5 s) or implement dual-link diversity (satellite + LTE). 4. Add link-quality monitoring to workbench: 'C2 latency trending [graph]; marginal links flagged for trial planning.' 5. Implement operator override with latency visibility: 'Current C2 latency: 1200 ms (degraded); recommend RTL in 30 s.'

title: Flight termination hard deadline 500 ms may not account for redundancy failover delays in safety-critical avionics
feature: Flight termination command to motor cutoff (500 ms hard deadline)
reasoning: Budget assumes single command path: operator → C2 → airborne parser → motor shutdown (500 ms). However, safety-critical avionics typically implement dual-redundant command processing: if primary channel fails, secondary channel activates, incurring additional 100–200 ms latency for failover detection and activation. Additionally, motor shutdown involves mechanical actuation (contactor release, brake engagement) which can be delayed by power-delivery latency (capacitor discharge, relay settling) up to 50–150 ms. For a swarm with 3+ aircraft, if command is broadcast to all, worst-case latency is the maximum across all aircraft (accounting for radio propagation variance). Combined, p99 could reach 700+ ms, exceeding hard deadline. Confidence is moderate-to-high based on avionics architecture patterns.
recommendation: 1. Measure flight termination latency on actual avionics hardware with single and dual-redundant command paths. Include radio propagation jitter (simulate multi-aircraft broadcast). 2. Baseline motor shutdown mechanical latency (measure motor coast-down time, relate to mission safety). 3. Re-evaluate hard deadline based on empirical data. If 500 ms unachievable, consider: (a) dual-independent termination systems (mechanical + electrical), (b) pre-termination auto-descent to lower altitude to reduce kinetic energy. 4. Implement termination command logging with precise timestamping; track end-to-end latency per flight. 5. Add acceptance test: 'Flight termination latency p99 <600 ms on fleet of [N] aircraft; failure mode: escalate to backup termination (mechanical).'
